title: Zionism Was Founded in 1897. Judaism Is 3,500 Years Old. The Conflation Has a Paper Trail.
teaser: Political Zionism was founded by Theodor Herzl at the First Zionist Congress in Basel in 1897. Judaism as a religious and cultural tradition is approximately 3,500 years old. The documented record of Jewish theologians, rabbis, and organized Jewish communities treating these two things as distinct -- and sometimes in direct opposition -- spans more than a century. ClownBinge reviewed the primary sources.
verifiedSource: Jewish Virtual Library; Central Conference of American Rabbis (Pittsburgh Platform, 1885); World Zionist Organization (Basel Program, 1897); U.S. House Judiciary Committee (Kenneth Stern Testimony, 2017); Jewish Voice for Peace
body:
Two numbers frame this record.

Judaism, as a religious and cultural tradition, is approximately 3,500 years old. Scholarly consensus dates early Israelite religious practice to at least the Bronze Age, with the Torah tradition placing its foundational events in the 13th century BCE. The religion's continuous practice -- documented in archaeology, canonical text, and an unbroken chain of rabbinical literature -- spans roughly 35 centuries.

Political Zionism is 127 years old. The First Zionist Congress convened in Basel, Switzerland, on August 29, 1897.

The distinction between these two things -- one a multi-millennial religious and cultural tradition, the other a 19th-century political movement -- was not invented by critics of Israel. It was documented by the founders of Zionism themselves, and by the rabbinical authorities who opposed the movement on explicitly theological grounds from its founding year forward. ClownBinge reviewed the primary record.

The Founding Document

Theodor Herzl published Der Judenstaat (The Jewish State) on February 14, 1896 -- not as a religious text, but as a political pamphlet. The subtitle described it explicitly: "Proposal of a Modern Solution for the Jewish Question." Herzl, an Austro-Hungarian journalist and playwright, was largely secular. His diary and correspondence document a man motivated by the political crisis of European antisemitism, not by religious mandate. His original proposals included diplomatic negotiations with the Ottoman Empire and the Argentine government as alternative locations for a Jewish homeland before Palestine was selected.

The platform adopted at the First Zionist Congress established what became known as the Basel Program: "Zionism seeks to establish a home for the Jewish people in Palestine secured under public law." The phrase "secured under public law" -- meaning through international diplomacy and recognized legal frameworks -- marked Zionism as a project of political negotiation. The document contains no invocation of divine command, prophetic fulfillment, or rabbinical authority.

The Jewish Response Before Zionism Was Founded

Twelve years before the First Zionist Congress, the Central Conference of American Rabbis adopted the Pittsburgh Platform of 1885, which established the foundational principles of American Reform Judaism. The Platform addressed the question of Jewish nationhood directly and without ambiguity:

"We consider ourselves no longer a nation, but a religious community, and therefore expect neither a return to Palestine, nor a sacrificial worship under the sons of Aaron, nor the restoration of any of the laws concerning the Jewish state."

This declaration predates political Zionism by twelve years. It represents the documented position of organized American Jewish religious leadership -- not a fringe opinion but the formally adopted platform of the Reform rabbinical conference -- that Jewish identity is a religious category, not a national-political one.

The Orthodox Rabbinical Record

Opposition to Zionism from within Orthodox Judaism was not a marginal reaction. The Neturei Karta, an Orthodox Jewish sect founded in Jerusalem in 1938 -- a decade before the establishment of the State of Israel -- explicitly opposes Zionism on Talmudic grounds. Their theological position holds that the establishment of a Jewish state before the arrival of the Messiah violates divine law as established in the Talmud's tractate Ketubot, which records three oaths against forcing the End of Days. This is not a political objection. It is a halakhic one, grounded in the same rabbinical tradition that predates Herzl by approximately 1,500 years.

The Satmar Hasidic dynasty -- one of the largest Hasidic communities in the world, with hundreds of thousands of adherents concentrated in New York and Israel -- formalized this theological opposition in the 1961 work Vayoel Moshe by Rabbi Yoel Teitelbaum. The text is the most comprehensive rabbinical refutation of Zionism in modern Orthodox literature, arguing on textual and halakhic grounds that Zionism violates the Torah's prohibition on forcing the messianic era through political action. It remains in print and in active use in Satmar communities today.

The Satmar community's opposition to Zionism is not a historical relic. It is a present, organized, majority position within one of the largest Hasidic groups in the United States. Members of the Satmar community live in the State of Israel while simultaneously rejecting its legitimacy as a Jewish state on religious grounds -- a distinction they document and defend through rabbinical literature.

The American Jewish Organizational Record

The American Council for Judaism was founded in 1942 by Reform Jewish rabbis and lay leaders who explicitly opposed the Zionist platform. At its peak the Council had approximately 15,000 members. Its founding position characterized Zionism as incompatible with the definition of Judaism as a religious community rather than a national-ethnic group. The Council was not a peripheral organization -- its founding members included prominent rabbis from established Reform congregations in major American cities.

Jewish Voice for Peace, a national Jewish American organization with chapters across the United States, explicitly distinguishes between Jewish identity and Zionism in its organizational platform. The organization affirms Jewish identity and community while rejecting Zionism as a political framework. It is led by and composed of Jewish Americans who draw the same line that Reform rabbis drew in 1885 and Orthodox rabbis drew in 1938.

The IHRA Definition and Its Own Author

The International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance (IHRA) Working Definition of Antisemitism has been adopted by numerous governments and institutions as a framework for identifying antisemitic conduct. Among its examples of potentially antisemitic behavior is "denying the Jewish people their right to self-determination" -- a clause that has been applied in some institutional contexts to criticism of Israeli government policy.

Kenneth Stern, the primary author of the IHRA definition, documented his concern about this application in public testimony and published writing. Testifying before the U.S. House Judiciary Committee on November 7, 2017, Stern stated that the definition he wrote was being misused as a tool to suppress political speech on college campuses, and that this use was contrary to his intent in drafting it. In a 2019 opinion piece, he described the application of his definition to silence criticism of Israeli policy as a "weaponization" of the framework.

The author of the most widely-used institutional definition of antisemitism documented, in congressional testimony, that the definition was being applied in ways that conflated criticism of Israeli policy with hatred of Jewish people -- the same conflation this record addresses.

What the Record Establishes

The documentary record on this question runs in one consistent direction across more than a century.

Judaism as a religious tradition preceded Zionism as a political movement by approximately 3,373 years at the time of the First Zionist Congress. The distinction between the two was made, documented, and institutionalized by Jewish theologians, rabbinical authorities, and organized Jewish communities from the moment Zionism was founded -- and, in the case of the Pittsburgh Platform, before it was founded.

The positions of the 1885 Pittsburgh Platform, the Neturei Karta, the Satmar Hasidim, the American Council for Judaism, and Jewish Voice for Peace are not identical. They represent a range of theological and political perspectives that agree on very little else. What they share is the documented conclusion -- grounded in Jewish religious text, rabbinical authority, and organizational history -- that Judaism and Zionism are not the same thing.

The conflation of the two has a paper trail. So does the record of Jewish voices, from Reform rabbis to Orthodox Hasidim to the author of the IHRA definition itself, rejecting that conflation.

ClownBinge publishes the record.
